execution_kernel_pattern: single-process agentic loop driven by a `Runner`: the loop calls the model, if the model returns a final output it stops, if it produces a handoff it switches the active agent and re-loops, if it produces tool calls they are executed and results fed back as new model input — continuing until a final output, a max_turns guard trips, or a guardrail tripwire fires. Built on top of OpenAI's Responses API (default) with adapters for Chat Completions and third-party providers. No graph compilation, no actor runtime, no shared message bus — orchestration emerges from the model's tool-call choices.

multi_agent_support.notes: Handoffs are the canonical multi-agent primitive and are *implemented as tool calls* — for an agent named `Refund Agent` the LLM is presented a synthetic tool `transfer_to_refund_agent`; invoking it switches the active agent inside the Runner loop and (by default) passes the entire conversation history to the receiving agent, with optional `input_filter` to strip or transform context. There is also `agent.as_tool()` for treating a sub-agent as a callable tool without yielding control (manager pattern). The same library ships realtime/voice agents and human-in-the-loop checkpoints. Compared to LangGraph's `Command(goto=...)` (which is a typed state-graph node-edge transition), OpenAI's handoff is a *runtime* tool-call observed by the Runner — no graph, no compiled state machine, no typed channels. Because the mechanism is just a tool call, handoffs depend entirely on the active model supporting function calling — they ride the LiteLLM bridge for free on Claude/Gemini *as long as* those providers honor tool-choice; OpenAI ships no portability matrix or test suite for this in mid-2026.

memory_model: Session-based: `Sessions` abstraction persists conversation history across Runner invocations, with pluggable backends (SQLite, SQLAlchemy, Redis, MongoDB, Dapr). No first-class vector/semantic memory primitive — long-term/RAG memory is left to the developer via tools.
supported_model_providers: Anthropic, Google, Mistral, etc. via LiteLLM extension (beta, best-effort)
observability_story: First-class. Built-in tracing is on by default: every Runner execution emits a trace tree (agent spans, generation spans, function/tool spans, handoff spans, guardrail spans, audio spans) viewable in the OpenAI Traces dashboard, and exporter hooks allow shipping to third-party observability backends (Logfire, AgentOps, Braintrust, Langfuse, Arize, W&B, MLflow, etc.). By default `trace_include_sensitive_data=True`, meaning LLM inputs/outputs and function-tool args/results are captured in `generation_span()` and `function_span()` and shipped to OpenAI's backend in batches via `BackendSpanExporter`. The trace endpoint is not user-configurable; orgs under OpenAI's Zero Data Retention (ZDR) policy cannot use tracing at all. Tracing data also feeds OpenAI's evals/fine-tuning/distillation tooling. This is the strongest out-of-the-box observability of any general-purpose Python agent framework as of mid-2026, but is paired with the strongest default-data-egress posture of any framework in this study.
typical_use_case: Production multi-agent workflows where a triage agent routes to specialist agents (customer support, refunds, billing); voice/realtime conversational agents using `gpt-realtime`; computer-use / browser-use agents via OpenAI's hosted computer-use tool; agentic RAG with hosted file-search and web-search tools; OpenAI-native deployments that want minimum framework overhead and maximum first-party feature coverage (Responses API streaming, structured outputs, hosted tools).
one_paragraph_differentiator: The OpenAI Agents SDK is the production successor to OpenAI's experimental Swarm and stakes out the **minimal-abstractions** end of the agent-framework spectrum: three primitives (Agent, Handoff, Guardrail) plus a Runner loop, no graph compiler, no role-and-task DSL, no actor runtime. Against LangGraph it trades typed-graph determinism and replayability for a much smaller surface area — handoffs are runtime tool-calls rather than `Command(goto=...)` state-graph transitions, so flow is emergent rather than explicit. Against AutoGen it rejects shared-chat group orchestration in favor of single-active-agent + delegation. Against CrewAI it has no role/process taxonomy. Against legacy Swarm it adds production-grade tracing, guardrails, sessions, realtime/voice, and an MCP-capable tool layer — Swarm is explicitly archived as 'experimentation' the SDK upgrades. The honest concern is portability: although LiteLLM/any-llm bridges expose 100+ providers, the SDK is deeply optimized for the OpenAI Responses API (hosted tools, computer-use, deferred tool search, ToolSearchTool, full tracing fidelity, reasoning summaries) and these features outright vanish on non-OpenAI providers — adopting the SDK is a soft bet on the OpenAI stack.
known_anti_patterns: OpenAI-first design: hosted tools (web-search, file-search, computer-use, ToolSearchTool, deferred-loading tools) and Responses-API features (structured outputs, reasoning summaries) are first-class only on OpenAI; LiteLLM bridge is officially 'beta / best-effort' and the docs themselves warn to 'validate the exact provider backend you plan to deploy if you depend on structured outputs, tool calling, usage reporting, or adapter-specific routing behavior' — some LiteLLM-backed providers don't populate SDK usage metrics at all

Tracing is *on by default* and ships LLM inputs/outputs + tool args/results to OpenAI's backend via `BackendSpanExporter` unless `OPENAI_AGENTS_DISABLE_TRACING=1`, `set_tracing_disabled(True)`, or per-run `RunConfig.tracing_disabled=True` is set; sensitive-data capture can be narrowed via `OPENAI_AGENTS_TRACE_INCLUDE_SENSITIVE_DATA=false` but the trace endpoint is not configurable, and ZDR-policy organizations cannot use built-in tracing at all — a real data-governance footgun

Handoffs-as-tools mean the LLM ultimately decides routing; no graph-level guarantees about which agent can hand off to which (must be enforced by the developer); also means handoff fidelity on non-OpenAI providers is entirely a function of that provider's tool-calling quality, with no first-party portability matrix

Default input guardrails run in parallel with the agent (`run_in_parallel=True`), so a tripwire can fire *after* tokens have been billed and tools have executed — must explicitly set `run_in_parallel=False` for true pre-flight blocking
No first-class persistent long-term/vector memory — Sessions store raw conversation history only
Single-process Runner; no native distributed/cross-language story (vs AutoGen Core's distributed runtime)

MCP support is client-only — Agents cannot themselves be exposed as MCP servers, and only the tools + prompts primitives are supported (resources and sampling are not in the documented surface as of mid-2026)
Successor to Swarm but Swarm-era patterns (lightweight context object, agent-as-function) leak through — some users still confuse the two on Stack Overflow
